content.body: Integration runs differentiation in reverse, and it splits over sums in exactly the same way. To integrate a polynomial, integrate each term on its own and add the results — the sum rule again.
Each term uses the reverse power rule: add 1 to the exponent, then divide by the new exponent. So $x^2 + x$ integrates to:
Integrate term by term — reverse the power rule on each. Drag the antiderivative of each term into the blanks to build $F(x)$ for $f(x) = x^3 + x^2 + x$.

feedback.correct: Right — reverse the power rule on each: $x^3 \to \frac{x^4}{4}$, $x^2 \to \frac{x^3}{3}$, and $x \to \frac{x^2}{2}$, so $F(x) = \frac{x^4}{4} + \frac{x^3}{3} + \frac{x^2}{2}$.
feedback.incorrect: Add 1 to each exponent, then divide by that new exponent.

content.body: One subtlety: an antiderivative isn't unique. The derivative of any constant is 0, so you can tack any number onto $\frac{x^3}{3}$ and it still differentiates back to $x^2$. That means $\frac{x^3}{3}$, $\frac{x^3}{3} + 1$, and $\frac{x^3}{3} - 7$ are all antiderivatives of $x^2$.
To capture the whole family at once, we add a constant of integration $C$. An integral with no limits — an indefinite integral — stands for every antiderivative together:
So the very first example is, more precisely, $\int (x^2 + x)\,dx = \frac{x^3}{3} + \frac{x^2}{2} + C$ — a single $C$ covers the constant for the whole sum.
Which expression represents every antiderivative of $f(x) = 3x^2$ — the full indefinite integral $\int 3x^2\,dx$?

feedback.correct: Right — $\frac{d}{dx}(x^3) = 3x^2$, and the $+ C$ captures every antiderivative at once.
feedback.incorrect: Reverse the power rule to find one antiderivative, then add $C$ to include all of them.
feedback.hint: $3x^2 \to x^3$ (raise the exponent, divide the coefficient by it), then write $+ C$: $x^3 + C$.

content.body: To evaluate a definite integral, the Fundamental Theorem says: find an antiderivative $F$, then subtract its values at the endpoints, $F(b) - F(a)$. For polynomials, $F$ comes straight from the reverse power rule.
Here the constant of integration doesn't matter: it's the same at both endpoints, so it cancels — $(F(b) + C) - (F(a) + C) = F(b) - F(a)$. That's why definite integrals never need a $C$.
Here $F(x) = x^2 + x$ is an antiderivative of $2x + 1$, and evaluating it from 0 to 2 gives 6.
Your turn. Use the antiderivative $F(x) = x^2 + x$ to evaluate this integral as $F(3) - F(0)$.

feedback.incorrect: Evaluate $x^2 + x$ at 3 and at 0, then subtract.

content.body: The shaded region is the area under $f(x) = x + 1$ from 0 to $t$. Its antiderivative is $F(x) = \frac{1}{2}x^2 + x$. Find the value of $t$ for which this area equals 4, then drag the slider there.

feedback.incorrect: Set $\frac{1}{2}t^2 + t = 4$ and solve for $t$, then drag the slider there.

content.body: Coefficients ride along here too. Build an antiderivative of $f(x) = 6x^2$ — use the steppers to set the coefficient and exponent of $F(x)$.

feedback.correct: Right — raise the exponent to 3, then divide the coefficient by it: $6x^2 \to 6 \cdot \frac{x^3}{3} = 2x^3$. Differentiate $2x^3$ to check: back to $6x^2$.
feedback.incorrect: Add 1 to the exponent, then divide the coefficient by that new exponent: $6x^2 \to \frac{6}{3}x^3$.

content.body: You can now run calculus in both directions on any polynomial: differentiate term by term with the power rule, or integrate term by term with the reverse power rule — adding a constant of integration $C$ for an indefinite integral — and with the Fundamental Theorem, turn those antiderivatives into exact areas.
Derivatives, integrals, and the theorem that unites them — that's the heart of calculus. Nice work making it through!
Use $F(x) = x^2 + x$ to evaluate the integral of $2x + 1$ from 0 to 2.

feedback.incorrect: Evaluate $x^2 + x$ at 2 and 0, then subtract.

content.body: Coefficients come along for the ride. Build an antiderivative of $f(x) = 12x^3$ by setting the coefficient and exponent of $F(x)$.

feedback.incorrect: Raise the exponent by 1, then divide the coefficient by the new exponent: $\frac{12}{4}x^4$.

content.body: Evaluate the integral of $4x^3 + 2x$ from 0 to 1. (An antiderivative is $x^4 + x^2$.)

feedback.incorrect: Evaluate $x^4 + x^2$ at 1 and 0, then subtract.

content.body: Match each function with its antiderivative. Remember a constant integrates to a linear term.

feedback.incorrect: Integrate each term with the reverse power rule; a constant $c$ becomes $cx$.

content.body: Evaluate the indefinite integral below. With no limits, don't forget the constant of integration.

feedback.correct: Yes — $4x \to \frac{4}{2}x^2 = 2x^2$, and an indefinite integral always carries $+ C$: $2x^2 + C$.
feedback.incorrect: Reverse the power rule on $4x$, then add $+ C$ because there are no limits.
